subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Wellâdefined sections with numbered headings and summary tables
Content is relevant to the mathematical aims throughout
weaknesses: Minor digressions and some repetition interrupt flow
Verbose explanations and duplicated tables reduce conciseness
Formatting inconsistencies in tables and references
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three presentation strands were assessed at band 3: the exploration is coherent with clear logical development, well organized into introduction, body and conclusion, and mostly concise. However, repeated itinerary tables, overâlong paragraphs and formatting issues prevent a band 4. A score of 3/4 reflects solid structure with room for improved conciseness and consistency.

strengths: Consistent and mostly accurate use of mathematical notation (vectors, equations, graph notation)
Effective use of multiple representations (maps, tessellations, graphs, matrices)
Clear alignment between diagrams, tables and narrative
weaknesses: Formatting issues with footnotes and legends in tables and figures
Some representations could be simplified or more rigorously linked to text
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand of Mathematical Communication is at band 3: notation is mostly appropriate, representations are effective though not highly sophisticated, and communication is clear despite minor inconsistencies. The work does not yet demonstrate the seamless precision of band 4, so a score of 3/4 is justified.

strengths: Clear personal approach through framing the problem as a real family trip
Significant evidence of independent thinking in selecting and combining two geometric methods
Demonstrated creativity in iteratively revising the itinerary based on personal criteria
weaknesses: Relies largely on standard textbook algorithms without novel adaptation
Choice of key parameters (e.g. radius r=1.5) lacks deeper justification or sensitivity analysis
Personal narrative enriches context but could be more tightly integrated with mathematical insights
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All Personal Engagement strands sit at band 2: the candidate shows significant independent thinking, a clear personal approach and creativity beyond routine work, but does not reach the âoutstandingâ level of band 3. Hence a score of 2/3 appropriately reflects strong but not exceptional engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on limitations of Voronoi and timeâbased TSP
Evaluation tables effectively summarize strengths and weaknesses
Clear links between reflection and enhancements to the exploration
weaknesses: Reflection depth is moderate and could probe mathematical implications more deeply
Critical analysis is present but not sustained at a deep level
Connections to broader mathematical understanding could be expanded
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each Reflection strand was judged band 2: the student provides meaningful reflection and some critical analysis with clear connections to understanding, but does not yet demonstrate the substantial, deep critique required for band 3. A score of 2/3 aligns with these moderate but solid reflective efforts.

strengths: Sophisticated use of multiple courseâlevel methods (Voronoi diagrams, circle loci, MST and TSP)
Calculations are correct with only minor errors and demonstrate rigour
Thorough understanding shown through algorithmic explanations, bounding arguments and crossâchecks
weaknesses: Minor numerical inconsistencies (e.g. NaâSa weight mismatch)
Some derivations switch prematurely to decimal approximations
Scaling details and formal bounding justifications could be more explicit
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work goes beyond effective use (band 4) by combining complementary advanced methods, rigorous algorithmic reasoning and bounding strategies, meriting band 5. Minor errors prevent a full band 6, so a score of 5/6 accurately reflects sophisticated and mostly precise mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing a real family trip, defining parameters, and deciding to modify the itinerary shows significant independent thought. However, much of the mathematical path follows textbook algorithms without novel adaptation, so evidence is âsignificantâ not âoutstandingâ.

Explanations of each algorithm, justification of methodological choices and interpretation of results demonstrate good understanding throughout. The work shows confidence but not the depth or formal proof required for the top two bands; thus âgood understanding throughoutâ.

Calculations (midpoints, gradients, circle equations, path weights, MST totals) are mostly correct; spot-checks confirm arithmetic. Minor inconsistencies (e.g., NaSa edge ordering typo) constitute âminor errorsâ, so the strand fits âmathematics correct with minor errorsâ.

Content is largely relevant; all calculations, graphs and narrative relate to determining an optimal hotel or route. Nevertheless, explanations are verbose (many screenshots of every Primâs step) and some tables/definitions are repeated, so conciseness is not sustained throughout.

Appropriate notation (vectors, circle equations, edge notation, inequalities for bounds) is used for most of the work, and variables are defined. A few typographical slips (NaSa written âNaSaâ vs âSaNaâ) appear but do not impede meaning; thus language is âmostly appropriateâ.

Explanations generally align with diagrams/tables, and symbols are used consistently. A few mismatches (for example, NaSa vs SaNa edge order) cause minor inconsistencies, but overall clarity is maintained.

Voronoi diagrams, circle loci and graph-theoretic algorithms are all at or above SL level and directly relevant. They are applied purposefully to the context, with two complementary methods and a full TSP treatment, showing effective use though not exceptional rigour.

The student employs multiple representations effectively: annotated maps, Voronoi tessellation, concentric circles, weighted graphs and adjacency matrices. Each representation supports the argument, but the sophistication level (e.g., dynamic sliders, layered GIS) is moderate rather than advanced, so âeffectiveâ rather than âsophisticatedâ.

The student reflects on limitations of Voronoi non-uniformity and time-based TSP, and produces an evaluation table of strengths/weaknesses, showing meaningful reflection, but depth is moderate; discussion stops short of probing mathematical implications in great detail.

Selecting two complementary geometric methods, integrating Google-maps data, and iteratively revising the itinerary display creativity and initiative beyond a routine task, yet the techniques themselves are standard; hence âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

The exploration follows a clear sequence: rationale â aim â plan â Part A â Part B â conclusion â evaluation. Arguments build logically (e.g., Voronoi analysis leading to circle refinement, then to TSP modification). Minor digressions and some repetition (e.g., repeated itinerary tables) slightly interrupt flow, so it is coherent rather than exemplary.

Critical analysis appears when the student contrasts upper and lower bounds, justifies algorithm choice (Prim vs Kruskal) and critiques Google-Maps data. These analyses contain some critique but lack sustained, deep reasoning, fitting the âmeaningful analysis with some critiqueâ descriptor.

Reflections are linked to understandingâfor example, recognising that circle zones address Voronoi limitations and that itinerary changes improve feasibility. The connection is clear but not extensively developed, so band 2 is appropriate.

Distinct sections, numbered headings and sub-headings, and summary tables give a recognisable introduction-body-conclusion structure. Transitions are mostly smooth, yet occasional over-long paragraphs and duplicated tables hinder a seamless feelâhence âwell organisedâ rather than âexceptionalâ.

The candidateâs personal story frames the task, and decisions (e.g., radius 1.5 units, shifting sites to Day 3) are personally justified, indicating a clear personal approach driving the work. It enriches but does not fully âenhanceâ mathematical understanding.

annotations.comment: Table 15âs layout misplaces travelâtime estimates under incorrect day columns; realign time entries in a separate column or annotate clearly under each day.
The rationale effectively grounds the exploration in personal experience but is verbose and shifts focus; suggest condensing and focusing on the mathematical problem to improve conciseness.
The aim is stated clearly, but the long compound sentence could be split to improve clarity and focus on variables separately for each part; consider breaking into two sentences.
The plan lists clear steps, but list items could be more conciseâconsider referencing methods by name only and moving detailed descriptions to the body.
Definition of the Voronoi diagram is accurate and well articulated, demonstrating clear use of proper mathematical language.
The map overlay process is described but lacks specific grid dimensions or scaling rationale; include explicit grid dimensions or scale factors for reproducibility.
Table 2âs column headers do not clearly distinguish site codes from coordinates; align and label each column consistently to reduce confusion.
The footnote in Table 2 uses superscript poorly; replace the superscript notation with a properly formatted footnote to maintain consistency.
In the derivation of the perpendicular bisector, you switch to decimals after computing c; retain the exact fraction until final approximation to enhance mathematical rigor.
Table of perpendicular bisectors (Table 4) includes non-adjacent site pairs, making it unwieldy; streamline by showing only adjacent pairs to improve readability.
The Desmos equation |xââx|áµ + |yâây|áµ = min(...) is introduced without explaining its connection to the Voronoi verification; clarify why this expression confirms edge correctness.
The counting of sites per Voronoi cell is methodical, but the treatment of boundary points could be justified more rigorously; explicitly state inclusion rules.
The circleâzone radius r = 1.5 is chosen arbitrarily; include a sensitivity analysis or rationale to show how different radii affect your hotel selection.
In the circle analysis you include boundary points by default; specify whether points on the circumference satisfy â¤r or <r to ensure clarity.
The Travelling Salesman Problem introduction correctly defines the Hamiltonian cycle, but could explicitly note that you seek the minimumâweight cycle in your weighted graph.
The itinerary table for Day 1 and Day 2 is repeated here; reference the earlier table rather than duplicating it to maintain conciseness.
The weighted adjacency tables for Day 1 (Table 7) are comprehensive but could be simplified by displaying only one half of the symmetric matrix.
The bounding relation lower â¤ m â¤ upper is stated correctly, but the justification of how these bounds approximate the TSP deserves further depth.
In the nearestâneighbour sum mâ â¤ 18+3+21+41+24+78+66=251, the weight NaâSa was taken as 24, yet the adjacency table lists it as 22; please reconcile this discrepancy.
The sequence of edges for the upper bound is clearly stated, demonstrating effective application of the nearestâneighbour algorithm.
The MST calculation via Primâs algorithm is well explained with appropriate selection steps and yields a clear lower bound.
The modified itinerary table does not clearly separate the new Day 3 entriesâthe empty cells can be confusing; restructure to highlight the threeâday schedule.
The weighted graph for modified Day 2 (Fig. 23) lacks edgeâlabel legends; include weight labels directly on edges or a clear legend for interpretability.
When rejecting TeâBs in the Kruskal MST, you note the cycle but could link this to the formal tree definition (no cycles) to reinforce rigorous justification.
The strengths table effectively highlights key methodological decisions and verifies solution reliability, showing meaningful reflection.
The weaknesses table insightfully identifies limitations but could be enriched by proposing concrete strategies to mitigate each limitation.
References contain formatting inconsistencies (overlines, fractions, stray LaTeX); apply a consistent citation style (APA/MLA) to enhance presentation.
